findings.evidence: <main> at line 27 contains another <main> at line 28. Only one <main> landmark is valid per document.

findings.description: The PDF contains a detailed diagram/illustration of a shellstock tag with labeled arrows pointing to various parts of the tag (dealer name, certification number, harvest date, harvest area, etc.). The HTML conversion does not include this image/diagram at all.
findings.evidence: PDF page 1 shows a detailed annotated example shellstock tag with arrows and callout boxes explaining each field. The HTML page 1 renders all this information as plain text without the visual tag diagram.

findings.description: The PDF page 1 uses a complex layout with a central tag image surrounded by explanatory callouts/annotations on left and right sides, creating a multi-column visual arrangement. The HTML converts this to a single-column linear text flow, which fundamentally changes the visual structure and the relationship between annotations and tag fields.
findings.evidence: PDF has a central tag with left-side annotations (Harvest Date explanation, Harvest Area explanation, 90 Day tag retention notice, Notice that product must remain refrigerated, Consumer Advisory Notice) and right-side annotations (Dealer Name info, Certification Number info, Type of shellfish info, Space for Reshipper). HTML presents all of this as sequential plain text.

findings.description: The HTML adds a section heading 'Example Shellstock Tag with Explanations' that does not appear anywhere in the source PDF.
findings.evidence:
HTML contains

Example Shellstock Tag with Explanations

but no such heading exists in the PDF.

findings.description: The HTML adds a section heading 'Tag Format and Handling Requirements' that does not appear anywhere in the source PDF.
findings.evidence:
HTML contains

Tag Format and Handling Requirements

but no such heading exists in the PDF. The PDF presents these rules as standalone paragraphs without a section heading.
